lists.title: Best Skills for Code Review
lists.audience: Developers who want faster PR review, architecture feedback, and safer merge decisions.
lists.intro: These are the best starting points when you want an agent to review code, spot regressions, and enforce engineering standards instead of just generating code.

skills.name: superpowers
skills.why_pick: Battle-tested engineering workflow pack with strong review, planning, and execution discipline.
skills.best_for: Teams or solo developers who want repeatable review rituals rather than ad hoc prompting.
skills.watch_out: Medium precheck risk because workflow automation can execute or influence local changes; review what gets installed and enabled.

skills.name: Trail of Bits Security Skills
skills.why_pick: Best fit when code review needs real security depth instead of generic style feedback.
skills.best_for: Security-sensitive codebases, audits, and pre-release review passes.
skills.watch_out: Security tooling can be noisy and may require extra dependencies; treat findings as analyst input, not final truth.

skills.name: claude-code-agents
skills.why_pick: Good option if you want multiple audit-style roles and browser-assisted QA in the same workflow.
skills.best_for: Larger feature work where review includes implementation, QA, and fix loops.
skills.watch_out: Broader automation surface means more moving parts; review permissions before enabling full workflows.

lists.title: Best Skills for Security Audit
lists.audience: Users evaluating third-party code, risky integrations, or production-facing agent workflows.
lists.intro: When the main question is not “can it work?” but “should I trust it?”, start with security-focused skills and collections.

skills.name: Trail of Bits Security Skills
skills.why_pick: Strongest security-first brand and clearest fit for static analysis, code auditing, and vuln discovery.
skills.best_for: Serious code review, AppSec triage, and security-focused engineering teams.
skills.watch_out: Expect a steeper learning curve than general-purpose skill packs.

skills.name: skill-scanner
skills.why_pick: Directly useful as a scanning-oriented utility when the job is triage and inspection.
skills.best_for: Early-pass filtering of unknown or community-contributed skills.
skills.watch_out: Scanner output can produce false positives; always pair scanning with human review.

skills.name: parry
skills.why_pick: Good companion tool when your threat model includes prompt injection and unsafe tool I/O.
skills.best_for: Hook pipelines and agent setups exposed to untrusted text or web content.
skills.watch_out: Early-stage projects may change quickly, so validate maintenance health before depending on them.

lists.title: Best Skills for Research
lists.audience: Researchers, analysts, and technical users gathering information, comparing options, or building evidence-backed summaries.
lists.intro: Good research skills help an agent collect context, structure findings, and preserve useful notes instead of creating disposable output.

skills.name: claude-scientific-skills
skills.why_pick: Broad, high-value bundle for research, analysis, engineering, and finance-adjacent workflows.
skills.best_for: Users who need one flexible pack across multiple research styles.
skills.watch_out: Larger packs can be harder to evaluate quickly; focus on the specific subskills you plan to use.

skills.name: planning-with-files
skills.why_pick: Useful when research needs persistent plans, decision logs, and visible progress.
skills.best_for: Long-running analysis tasks with multiple branches and checkpoints.
skills.watch_out: Because it writes workflow artifacts, keep repository clutter and path scope under control.

skills.name: obsidian-skills
skills.why_pick: Excellent if your research workflow already lives in markdown notes or Obsidian.
skills.best_for: Knowledge capture, note synthesis, and turning research into reusable internal documentation.
skills.watch_out: Best value comes when your team already uses markdown-first knowledge systems.

lists.title: Best Skills for Debugging
lists.audience: Developers trying to isolate bugs, narrow root causes, and speed up fix loops.
lists.intro: The best debugging skills combine methodical workflows with enough automation to inspect, reproduce, and verify fixes.

skills.name: superpowers
skills.why_pick: Strong for disciplined debugging, planning, and execution loops in real codebases.
skills.best_for: Users who want a consistent way to investigate issues instead of improvising every session.
skills.watch_out: Review enabled commands and scripts before trusting it in sensitive repos.

skills.name: claude-tmux
skills.why_pick: Great for switching between multiple agent sessions while debugging parallel hypotheses.
skills.best_for: Power users running several debugging threads or long-lived coding sessions.
skills.watch_out: Adds operational complexity; best for advanced terminal users.

skills.name: claude-esp
skills.why_pick: Useful when you want visibility into hidden reasoning/tool behavior during a debugging run.
skills.best_for: Understanding what the agent is doing under the hood during fixes or failures.
skills.watch_out: Observability tools may expose more session detail than casual users need.

lists.title: Best Skills for Documentation
lists.audience: Teams turning raw implementation work into docs, handbooks, changelogs, or reusable knowledge assets.
lists.intro: These skills are best when the output needs to be understandable by humans later, not just useful to the agent right now.

skills.name: internal-comms
skills.why_pick: Strong official option for clear internal writing, FAQs, and status-style communication.
skills.best_for: Teams documenting launches, decisions, and rollout notes.
skills.watch_out: It helps with format and clarity, but you still need strong source material.

skills.name: Book Factory
skills.why_pick: Good example of long-form structured writing workflows rather than one-shot generation.
skills.best_for: Large documentation projects and multi-step writing pipelines.
skills.watch_out: More process-heavy than most teams need for lightweight docs work.

skills.name: obsidian-skills
skills.why_pick: Strong markdown-native fit for teams building reusable internal knowledge bases.
skills.best_for: Documentation systems that grow over time instead of one-off pages.
skills.watch_out: Most useful if your docs stack already maps well to markdown and local files.

lists.title: Best Skills for Automation
lists.audience: Operators and builders who want agents to execute repeatable workflows, not just generate suggestions.
lists.intro: Automation skills are the most powerful—and the easiest to misuse—so usefulness and risk need to be judged together.

skills.name: AgentSys
skills.why_pick: High-value automation system for end-to-end workflows, reviews, cleanup, and investigations.
skills.best_for: Teams that want workflow leverage across development operations.
skills.watch_out: Automation breadth means more permission surface; start narrow before enabling full workflows.

skills.name: Dippy
skills.why_pick: Helps reduce permission fatigue by auto-approving safer bash commands using structured parsing.
skills.best_for: Users who want faster agent execution without turning safety fully off.
skills.watch_out: Auto-approval is inherently sensitive; understand the safety rules before relying on it.

skills.name: sudocode
skills.why_pick: Useful for repo-local orchestration where execution should follow a shared project process.
skills.best_for: Teams coordinating specs, tasks, and agent execution inside a codebase.
skills.watch_out: Process tooling can add overhead if the team is still small or informal.

lists.title: Best Skills for Browser & UI Testing
lists.audience: Users who need agents to open apps, click through flows, and verify UI behavior instead of reasoning only from code.
lists.intro: These skills are best when correctness depends on what actually happens in a browser or simulator.

skills.name: webapp-testing
skills.why_pick: Clean official fit for UI verification and local web app testing.
skills.best_for: Regression checks, smoke tests, and reproduction of UI bugs.
skills.watch_out: Browser automation can touch real user state or accounts if not isolated.

skills.name: playwright-skill
skills.why_pick: Good general-purpose browser automation option with direct practical value.
skills.best_for: Teams that want hands-on automation over local or staging web flows.
skills.watch_out: Use clean environments; browser tools can leak state across sessions if unmanaged.

skills.name: ios-simulator-skill
skills.why_pick: Rare practical option for testing mobile workflows through an agent-assisted loop.
skills.best_for: iOS developers who need automation and validation inside simulator-driven flows.
skills.watch_out: Environment setup is heavier than typical desktop/browser skills.

lists.title: Best Skills for DevOps & Deployment
lists.audience: Developers and operators shipping code, managing infra, and keeping deployments reproducible.
lists.intro: The best DevOps skills reduce toil, but they should also make infra actions more legible, not more opaque.

skills.name: cc-devops-skills
skills.why_pick: One of the clearest infra-focused packs for generating and validating deployment-related work.
skills.best_for: Engineers who routinely work with cloud, IaC, and delivery pipelines.
skills.watch_out: Infra automation deserves careful review because mistakes can affect production resources.

skills.name: compound-engineering-plugin
skills.why_pick: Good for disciplined engineering workflows where lessons, guardrails, and execution patterns matter.
skills.best_for: Teams that care about operational quality and institutional memory.
skills.watch_out: Works best when the team is willing to adopt its operating model, not just cherry-pick commands.

skills.name: claude-codex-settings
skills.why_pick: Useful bridge pack when your workflows touch cloud platforms and developer services.
skills.best_for: Polytool teams working across GitHub, cloud, and external services.
skills.watch_out: Service integrations can expand the credential and permission surface quickly.

lists.title: Best Skills for Knowledge & Memory
lists.audience: Users who want agents to accumulate context, preserve decisions, and avoid re-solving the same problem repeatedly.
lists.intro: Skills in this bucket are most valuable when the work lasts longer than one session and context quality matters as much as raw output speed.

skills.name: obsidian-skills
skills.why_pick: Excellent fit for markdown-native memory, note operations, and long-term reusable knowledge.
skills.best_for: Writers, researchers, and teams using Obsidian or markdown repositories.
skills.watch_out: Local file interaction means users should understand what folders are in scope.

skills.name: planning-with-files
skills.why_pick: Great for agents that need persistent plans, checkpoints, and explicit task state.
skills.best_for: Long-running execution threads with lots of iteration and follow-up.
skills.watch_out: Without cleanup, planning artifacts can become clutter instead of leverage.

skills.name: Context Engineering Kit
skills.why_pick: Strong conceptual fit when the real problem is giving the agent better context, not more brute-force prompting.
skills.best_for: Advanced users tuning result quality and context efficiency.
skills.watch_out: More meta than turnkey; best for users willing to shape their own system.

lists.title: Best Skills for Getting Started
lists.audience: New users who want a small set of high-signal starting points instead of a giant marketplace dump.
lists.intro: If you only install a few things first, choose packs that are widely referenced, easy to understand, and broadly useful.

skills.name: skills
skills.why_pick: Best official starting point with strong baseline credibility and examples.
skills.best_for: Anyone learning what a mature skill repository looks like.
skills.watch_out: Even official collections can include powerful capabilities; read what each skill actually does.

skills.name: awesome-claude-code
skills.why_pick: Best discovery surface for understanding the landscape before choosing what to install.
skills.best_for: Users who want breadth and discovery more than immediate automation.
skills.watch_out: Awesome lists help you discover options, but do not replace repo-level due diligence.

skills.name: superpowers
skills.why_pick: High-signal pack for people who want immediate workflow value once they’re ready for more than basics.
skills.best_for: Developers who want practical engineering leverage fast.
skills.watch_out: It is more opinionated and operational than a beginner-only starter pack.
